content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         322-6444


August 11, 1982


Honorable Alicemarie H. Stotler
Judge of the Superior Court
700 Civic Center Drive West
Santa Ana, CA 92701

Re: Our Advice No. A-82-144

Dear Judge Stotler:

    The Fair Political Practices Commission received your
Statement of Economic Interests, Form 721, for the period
of January 1, 1981, through December 31, 1981. Schedule H
(Income to Business Entities) required you to specify the
names of those clients of your husband's law firm1/ who
provided so much in fees to the firm that your community
property interest in your husband's pro rata share of the
fees from each client was $10,000 or more.2/

    On your Statement, you refused to comply with the
disclosure requirements of Schedule H and stated two
reasons for this refusal. First, you stated that you have
no information as to the identity of such clients and you
do not have the means to compel disclosure of this infor-
mation. Secondly, you stated that, to the extent that your
husband has provided you with information about his clients
and the fees paid to him, you feel that such information is
privileged pursuant to the marital privilege (Evidence Code
Sections 980, et seq., and 950 through 955).


_____________________

1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated.

2/  Thus, you are required to report those clients who
provided your husband with $20,000 in fees.

Honorable Alicemarie H. Stotler
August 11, 1982
Page 2


     I will initially address your second assertion.  The
provisions of the Political Reform Act, requiring dis-
closure of the names of clients, have been upheld by the
courts.  Your claim of a marital privilege is not a basis
for nondisclosure.  As the California Supreme Court stated
in Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 Cal. 3d 772, 783:

     We find no basis for concluding that... [the
disclosure of the names of customers and clients]
necessarily results in an unwarranted and
unconstitutional intrusion into protected zones of
privacy..."

     Similarly, in County of Nevada v. MacMillan (1974) 11 Cal.
3d 662, 672, the Court, in considering the statute which
was the predecessor to the Political Reform Act, held that
the right of privacy does not prevail over the right of the
public to an honest and impartial government, as imple-
mented by legislation requiring public officials to
disclose their financial interests.  Also, in Barry v.
Bales, Marin County Superior Court, No. 83978, June 20,
1977, that court ordered the defendant, Judge David R.
Baty, to disclose the separate property investments,
interests in real property and income of his wife, if he
knew them of his own knowledge and, if not, to make a good
faith effort, using reasonable diligence, to ascertain and
declare such interests.

     Thus, as to those clients about whom your husband has
provided you with information, if the amount of fees paid
by the clients meets the reporting threshold, you are
required to report their names.

     As to your first assertion, you refused to disclose
your husband's clients because you said that you do not
know the names of the clients and you do not have the means
to compel disclosure of this information.  The Political
Reform Act requires public officials to exercise reasonable
diligence in completing their Statement of Economic
Interests (Section 81004).  In reporting the names of
clients of a spouse's business, you must report those
clients' names of which you know or have reason to know.

     Reasonable diligence, of course, requires you to
disclose all of the names which you do know through
conversations with your husband, social gatherings, or
other channels.  You must also expressly ask your spouse
for the names of clients and you must check all records
which are available to you.

Honorable Alicemarie H. Stotler
August 11, 1982
Page 3


     You need not absolutely know that a client supplied
your husband's firm with so much in fees that the reporting
threshold is met, for it is sufficient that you have reason
to know this.3/ For instance, if your husband has had a
long-time client who has, in the past, consistently
supplied the firm with the requisite amount of fees to
require reporting, you must report the client if you have
reason to believe that the client is still with the firm
and paid a similar amount of fees to the firm.

     I hope I have clarified the Act's requirements for
you.  I am enclosing a supplemental Schedule H so that you
may amend your statement to make the appropriate disclosure
of clients' names.  Please complete the schedule within 30
days and send it to the Clerk of the Court, who will retain
a copy and forward the original to the Commission.  If you
have any questions, please feel free to contact me at
916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km

_____________________

     3/  This letter overrules a requirement, previously
articulated in an advice letter to Henry Simon,
November 16, 1976, which made an official disclose all of
the clients' names he or she knew, even if the official did
not know whether the amount of fees paid by the clients met
the disclosure threshold, in situations where the
official's spouse refused to indicate which clients paid
the requisite amount of fees.

     At the time of the Simon letter the reporting threshola
for clients' names was $1,000.  Pursuant to the ruling in
Hays v. Wood (1979) 25 Cal.3d 772, this threshold was
raised to its current $10,000 level.  In light of this
increase, official's need not disclose the names of all
clients, but need only disclose the names of clients whom
they know, or have reason to know, paid more than the
threshold amount.

sections.question_synthetic: Must Judge Stotler disclose the names of her husband's law firm clients on Schedule H of her Statement of Economic Interests, and can she refuse disclosure based on marital privilege or lack of knowledge?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Judge Stotler must disclose client names that she knows or has reason to know about, and marital privilege does not excuse disclosure. She must exercise reasonable diligence by asking her spouse, checking available records, and reporting clients she knows through various channels if fees meet the $10,000 threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format. No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but question and conclusion are clearly articulated in the body text. Document addresses Schedule H reporting requirements and contains detailed legal analysis with case citations. Includes directive to amend statement within 30 days.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must Judge Stotler disclose the names of her husband's law firm clients on Schedule H of her Statement of Economic Interests, and can she refuse disclosure based on marital privilege or lack of knowledge?

CONCLUSION: Judge Stotler must disclose client names that she knows or has reason to know about, and marital privilege does not excuse disclosure. She must exercise reasonable diligence by asking her spouse, checking available records, and reporting clients she knows through various channels if fees meet the $10,000 threshold.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Judge Alicemarie H. Stotler that she must disclose the names of her husband's law firm clients on her Statement of Economic Interests when those clients paid fees meeting the reporting threshold, rejecting her claims of marital privilege and lack of knowledge.
